data.title: KKR to Acquire $400 Million Controlling Stake in Indian Cancer Hospital Chain
data.description:
KKR has agreed to acquire a $400 million controlling stake in India's Healthcare Global Enterprises from CVC, a private markets manager, the companies said Sunday.

Under the terms of the agreement, KKR's Asia Fund IV will acquire up to 54% of equity in the 2,500-bed oncology hospital chain from CVC at a price of 445 Indian rupees ($5.13) per share, the companies said.

In accordance with Indian securities regulations, KKR will then make an offer to purchase additional Healthcare Global Enterprises shares from public shareholders, giving it a stake of between 54% and 77%, the companies said.

Healthcare Global Enterprises founder BS Ajaikumar will be named non-executive chairman.

The transaction is expected to close by Q3, the companies said.

data.title: India non-bank lender IIFL Finance may see a drop in annual earnings, Fitch says
data.description:
Indian non-bank lender IIFL Finance will likely see its annual earnings fall from a year earlier due to reduced net interest margins and higher credit costs, Fitch Ratings said on Monday.

Earlier this month, IIFL Finance reported an 8% fall in loan assets under management to 714.10 billion rupees ($8.24 billion) for the first nine months of the fiscal year that ends in March.

The company reported a loss of 144.6 million rupees, not factoring in taxes and a one-time gain, in the nine-month period, against a profit of 5.58 billion rupees a year ago.

Nirmal Jain, managing director at IIFL, flagged a challenging quarter on asset quality stress in its microfinance segment, adding the gold loan segment faced pressure as the company struggled to regain customers.

In March 2024, the Indian central bank ordered IIFL Finance to stop disbursing gold loans, citing "material supervisory concerns". It lifted that ban in September.

IIFL has also faced other challenges, including a search by tax authorities at three of its companies.

Fitch Ratings said weak loan quality and narrower interest spreads will impact the company's near-term performance, which could also pose downside risks to its ratings.

Fitch's current rating on IIFL Finance is 'B+' with a stable outlook. Its assessment of the company's performance is based on the nine-month earnings.

IIFL's performance mirrors weakness in microfinance and other unsecured segments across companies, while credit costs remain high, the rating agency said.

($1 = 86.6880 Indian rupees)

data.title: Bharat Forge Unit Kalyani Powertrain Collaborates With AMD
data.description:
Bharat Forge Ltd :

  • BHARAT FORGE LTD - KALYANI POWERTRAIN COLLABORATES WITH AMD

  • BHARAT FORGE LTD - COLLABORATION TO INTRODUCE ADVANCED EPYC SERVER TECHNOLOGIES TO INDIA

data.title: Italy's Saipem, Norways' Subsea 7 to create European energy services leader
data.description:
Italy's Saipem has agreed to merge with Norwegian rival Subsea 7 in an all-share deal that will create a leading European supplier of onshore and offshore energy services, the two companies said late on Sunday.

Shares in Subsea 7 rose as much as 9% in early trade, and gained 5% on the Oslo bourse by 0830 GMT. Saipem opened up more than 5%, later paring gains to rise 1.4%.

The combined group, to be renamed Saipem7, will have a total order backlog of 43 billion euros ($45 billion), revenue of about 20 billion euros and core earnings of more than 2 billion euros, they said.

"The combined business will create cost savings and a strengthened integrated offering, particularly in offshore," Citi analysts said in a note for clients, adding the merger could boost payouts in 2025 and 2026 for Saipem and Subsea7's shareholders.

The transaction is expected to generate annual benefits, mainly through cost savings, of around 300 million euros from the third year after completion, thanks to fleet optimisation, unified procurement, sales and marketing operations.

Subsea7 shareholders will receive 6.688 Saipem shares for each share they hold, the companies said in a statement.

($1 = 0.9547 euros)

data.title: Singapore January core inflation at 0.8% in smallest annual rise since 2021
data.description:
Singapore's key consumer price gauge rose 0.8% in January from a year earlier, lower than economists' forecasts and the smallest rise in more than three years, official data showed on Monday.

The core inflation rate, which excludes private road transport and accommodation costs, compared with a 1.5% forecast by a Reuters poll of economists.

Headline inflation was 1.2% in annual terms in January, lower than economists' forecasts of 2.15%.

Statistics Singapore said the consumer price index had been rebased to 2024 from 2019. The annual change in the headline inflation rate in December was revised to 1.5% from 1.6%.

Core inflation fell from a peak of 5.5% in early 2023 and eased further late last year. The January rate was the smallest rise since June 2021 when it rose by 0.6%.

OCBC economist Selena Ling said the softer-than-expected core inflation could lead to further easing of Singapore's monetary policy in its next review in April.

Ling said: "While trade frictions may escalate and prove inflationary for economies like the U.S., the impact on Singapore's imported prices is likely to be mitigated by disinflationary drags due to weaker global demand."

The central bank loosened monetary policy last month for the first time since 2020.

Singapore forecasts GDP growth to moderate to ease to 1%-3% in 2025 from 4.4% in 2024, and core inflation to be at 1.0% to 2.0% this year.

data.title: Conference: food safety - one of the priorities of the Polish presidency of the EU
data.description:
WARSAW, Poland, Feb. 24, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Since Poland joined the European Union the value of exports of agri-food products has increased: from €5.4 billion in 2004 to €54 billion in 2023, said Stefan Krajewski, Secretary of State at the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development, the guest of honor at a seminar of the Polish Presidency of the EU Council titled “Safety, Europe!”, organized by the Agricultural and Food Quality Inspection (GIJHARS).

“The food that reaches us should be safe. Cooperation between the ministry and many institutions is extremely important so that we can be sure that food meets all standards. It is also important that we operate within the EU and exchange information,” Minister Krajewski said.

The event "Safety, Europe!” The Role of the FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius Commission in Food Safety and the Polish Presidency of the EU was held under the patronage of the Polish Presidency of the Council of the EU, with Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development Czesław Siekierski acting as honorary patron.

The purpose of the event contained a general presentation of the activities of the Agricultural and Food Quality Inspection and its tasks in the field of international cooperation with particular emphasis on its activities at the FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius Commission. The seminar also aimed to discuss the role of the Inspection during the Polish Presidency of the Council of the EU.

Dr. Przemysław Rzodkiewicz, Chief Inspector of the Agricultural and Food Quality Inspection, said that the Inspection, by virtue of its competence to coordinate Poland’s cooperation with selected international organizations, is the leading institution in the work of the Council of the EU’s Task Force on International Food and Agricultural Questions.

“As the presidency, we represent the entire EU at international organizations, in the case of the Inspection it is the FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius Commission,” Dr. Rzodkiewicz pointed out.

The Codex Alimentarius Commission is the largest intergovernmental organization dealing with food and international trade of food products.

During the first half of 2025, the Inspection experts a.o. will chair nine meetings of EU member state representatives and European Commission experts in Brussels. They will be aimed at preparing member states to represent EU interests at the FAO/WHO global food standards program.

Source: GIJHARS

CONTACT:

tel. 22 25 57 800

A video accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/7bcb171d-e5e1-4e83-8cae-852c37f37db5

data.title: Italian Stocks Kick Off Week Lower
data.description:
The FTSE MIB fell by 0.4% to around 38,250 in early trading on Monday, starting the week on a negative note, as investors digested the results of the German election.

Friedrich Merz's CDU/CSU conservative alliance has won the general election with 28.6% of the vote, according to preliminary results.

The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) secured 20.8%, its strongest result to date at the federal level, while the SPD’s share of the vote dropped to 16.4%, placing third.

On the corporate front, notable decliners included Prysmian (-3.8%), Tenaris (-1.6%), Banca Generali (-1.5%), and STMicroelectronics (-1.2%).

Meanwhile, Saipem gained 1.2% after announcing its merger with Norwegian rival Subsea 7 in a $4.63 billion deal.

data.title: Caleffi Starts Share Buyback Program On February 24, 2025, Maximum Value EUR 100,000
data.description:
Caleffi SpA :

  • STARTS SHARE BUYBACK PROGRAM ON FEBRUARY 24, 2025, MAXIMUM VALUE EUR 100,000

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0854 GMT - European banks continued to surprise with their fourth-quarter revenue and preprovision profit beating estimates and confirming the sector's earnings momentum, Jefferies says in a research note. The pretax profit of the 28 lenders in the broker's coverage surpassed market estimates by around 5% thanks to higher income, in-line provisions and despite slightly higher costs, analysts write. They note that consensus might have had become too bearish on net interest income after a third-quarter sector miss. The analysts flagged that the sector beat pretax profit views for no less than 18 quarters in a row, but that the overperformance had decelerated against Q2 and Q3 of 2024. The Stoxx 600 banking subindex has gained 18% since the start of the year.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - Southern European banks reported another quarter of solid earnings beats, leading Keefe, Bruyette & Woods to lift its target prices by 15% on average, the brokerage says in a research note. Guidance for this year was generally positive with most lenders seeing a pick up in loan growth, analysts Hugo Cruz and Ben Maher write. They add that the M&A landscape is getting increasingly complicated but deals can be accretive for most parties if management teams are disciplined. "A lower cost of equity is consistent with the emerging theme of European banks' rerating...and we think many banks in Southern Europe are a good way to play it, given their still low [price to earnings ratios]," they write. KBW raises Santander stock to outperform and cuts CaixaBank's to market perform. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - Hong Kong shares closed lower, reversing earlier gains. The benchmark Hang Seng Index declined 0.6% to 23341.61 and the Hang Seng Tech Index lost 1.2%. Pharmaceutical and technology stocks led losses. WuXi AppTec fell 10% and Wuxi Biologics dropped 9.0%. Kuaishou Technology and Tencent declined 5.6% and 3.8%, respectively. Meanwhile, among top gainers, Longfor Group added 5.9% and China Mengniu Dairy rose 4.7%. China Overseas Land & Investment gained 4.0%. Investors are watching for the Hong Kong budget due Wednesday and China's Two Sessions meeting starting next week. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0822 GMT - Investor spirits have been lifted on the hope that the election win by Germany's conservatives will pull the country out of an economic stupor and bolster collective defense spending, Susannah Streeter, head of money and markets at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. Friedrich Merz, the leader of the CDU/CSU party, has pledged to relax fiscal rules, increase defense spending and boost the economy with much needed investment, she writes. With Russia's aggression in Europe set to remain an unrelenting black cloud, political factions may pull together to boost contributions to NATO, she adds. The German DAX trades up 0.3% at 22359 points. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - Puig Brands should be able to continue to outperform its rivals, after recent positive messaging from fragrance peers confirming the strength of this category, Citi analysts say in a note. The Spanish fashion and beauty group is scheduled to publish results for 2024 on Thursday after markets close. The group is likely to stick with its existing medium-term guidance, without guiding on 2025, the analysts say. Looking ahead, the company should benefit from an easier comparison base in 1H, as well as positive comments from peers on the fragrance category and a reduced headwind from the withdrawal of a Charlotte Tilbury product, Citi says. Shares are up 1.3% at 18.47 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - SATS Ltd. may face earnings volatility over the next one to two years stemming from global cargo demand becoming more sensitive to escalating trade tensions, CGS International analysts Tay Wee Kuang and Lim Siew Khee say in a note. Management noted some knee-jerk order cancellations in January as the U.S. mulled the repeal of the de minimis provision for low-value parcels from China, they say. The brokerage cuts its FY 2025-FY 2027 EPS forecasts for the air-cargo handler by 4.1%-7.5% amid an uncertain macroeconomic outlook. It also lowers its target to S$4.35 from S$4.40 while maintaining an add rating. Shares are last at S$3.20. (hoishan.chan@wsj.com)

0806 GMT - The possible merger of Saipem and Subsea7 will create cost savings and a strengthened integrated offering, particularly in offshore, Citi analyst Kate O'Sullivan writes. The combined business will be renamed Saipem7 when the deal closes in the second half of 2026. "We think the benefits of oil services consolidation such as this include cost rationalization, tightening utilizations and improved access to integrated offerings." Additionally Citi sees a potential significant boost to shareholder distributions in 2025/2026 for Saipem and Subsea7 shareholders based on the transaction memo of understanding. Citi has buy/high risk ratings on both Saipem and Subsea7. Subsea7 shares closed at 181.60 Norwegian kroner and Saipem at 2.32 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Nestle is expected to deliver a profit margin this year in line with its guidance of 16% or more, given the resilient performance of its coffee and chocolate confectionery businesses, RBC Capital Markets analysts James Edwardes Jones and Wassachon Udomsilpa say in a note. Nestle's coffee and chocolate confectionery segments account for around 30% of the group's total sales and the recent commodity inflation surge must have added intense pressure on their profitability, the analysts say. However, both categories have shown resilience over the last 20 years, and the powdered and liquid beverages business hasn't posted a sales volume decline since at least 2003. For 2025, RBC anticipates real internal growth--Nestle's metric for sales volume--in powdered and liquid beverages and a 4% decline in chocolate confectionery, with price inflation growth of 6% and 8%, respectively. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Dollar purchases ahead of month-end rebalancing of portfolios look weak, Barclays analysts say in a note. The dollar has come off its recent peak as positive sentiment surrounding the currency has retraced, they say. This largely increases asset values in dollars outside the U.S. and raises the need to buy dollars at month-end for rebalancing. The outperformance of European equities compared to the U.S. and appreciation of the Japanese yen against the dollar "contributed to a moderate dollar-buying signal against both currencies at month-end," they say. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 106.409 after hitting a two-and-a-half-month low of 106.126 overnight. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - The highlight of Bank of Ireland's update is the very bullish guidance, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Irish lender's annual print. "The results were nothing special, but the outlook is a big positive and [we] would expect shares to perform well," analyst Andrew Stimpson writes. The bank's adjusted return on tangible equity guidance of more than 17% by 2027 materially surpasses any expectations, he says. Consensus for 2026's RoTE is only 13.5%, he notes. The 3% to 4% annual growth in deposit and loan volumes guided for the next three years is also welcome, the analyst says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - Prosus's all-cash offer for Just Eat Takeaway will likely proceed, RBC Capital Markets analysts Wassachon Fon Udomsilpa and Richard Chamberlain write in a note. The Amsterdam-listed investment group has reach a conditional agreement to buy the food-delivery company for 4.1 billion euros in cash. "While there is a possibility of a counter bid, we believe Prosus is in a good position considering its offer price having over 60% premium to Just Eat Takeaway.com's latest share price," the analysts say. The offer is unanimously recommended by Just Eat's management and supervisory board. Just Eat shares closed at 12.43 euros on Friday and Prosus shares closed at 45.98 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0759 GMT - Shares in Subsea7 and Saipem should both react positively this morning after the companies announced a proposed merger, RBC Capital Markets analyst Victoria McCulloch writes. The enlarged business would have a 43 billion euro backlog, revenue of around 20 billion euros, and EBITDA of over 2 billion euros, McCulloch says. "The deal terms put a premium on Subsea7's market cap relative to Saipem based on Subsea 7 distributing 450 million euros in cash to shareholders prior to the deal's completion." Based on the 450 million euro cash distribution and the implied 50/50 split of the combined businesses, RBC thinks there's a premium being attributed to Subsea7's business. This already trades at a higher valuation multiple. Subsea7 shares closed at 181.60 Norwegian kroner and Saipem at 2.32 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: European Banks' Quarterly Beats Confirm Momentum — Market Talk
data.description: European banks continued to surprise with their fourth-quarter revenue and preprovision profit beating estimates and confirming the sector's earnings momentum, Jefferies says in a research note. The pretax profit of the 28 lenders in the broker's coverage surpassed market estimates by around 5% thanks to higher income, in-line provisions and despite slightly higher costs, analysts write. They note that consensus might have had become too bearish on net interest income after a third-quarter sector miss. The analysts flagged that the sector beat pretax profit views for no less than 18 quarters in a row, but that the overperformance had decelerated against Q2 and Q3 of 2024. The Stoxx 600 banking subindex has gained 18% since the start of the year.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Hong Kong's Tri-Wall to open 31 mln euro plant in Bulgaria
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), February 24 (SeeNews) - The Bulgarian unit of Hong Kong-based packaging manufacturer Tri-Wall Limited is investing 60 million levs ($32.1 million/30.7 million euro) in a new corrugated cardboard and packaging plant near Bulgaria's second-biggest city of Plovdiv, the innovation ministry said.

The production facility will be located in the Trakia Economic Zone and is expected to create 150 jobs, the ministry said in a press release on Friday.

To support the project, the innovation ministry has signed contracts for the construction of technical infrastructure with the investor and the Maritsa municipality where the plant will be located. As part of the deal, the ministry has made an advance payment of 4 million levs to the municipality.

Tri-Wall Limited has more than 100 facilities across Japan, Southeast Asia, China and Europe, according to its LinkedIn profile. In Southeast Europe, it also has a subsidiary in Romania, its website shows.

(1 euro = 1.95583 levs)

data.title: South African Rand Hovers Around 1-Week High
data.description:
The South African rand traded around 18.3 per USD, hovering around one-week highs, amid a softer dollar and as traders awaited a batch of domestic data.

Consumer price inflation, producer price data, and trade and budget balance figures set to be released this week will be closely watched for insights into the country's economy.

Meanwhile, load-shedding returned to the highest level in a year in South Africa after multiple generating units at the state utility Eskom failed.

data.title: Marico Launches New Oats Product
data.description:
Fast-moving consumer goods company Marico has launched a new oats product, named Saffola Cuppa Oats, according to a Monday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Saffola Cuppa Oats will be available in two flavors in leading retail stores and quick-commerce platforms in India, the filing said.

Shares of the company were down nearly 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Silver Ridge Prices Private Placement Shares
data.description:
Silver Ridge set the issue price of the ninth tranche of its private placement shares at 0.45 ringgit apiece, according to a Friday Malaysian bourse filing.

The management services provider proposed to issue up to 30% of the total number of its issued shares to private investors.

The issue price represents a 19.28% discount to the five-day volume-weighted average market price of the company's shares up to and including Feb. 20.

data.title: Germany's Merz Could Have Little Room to Boost Defense Spending — Market Talk
data.description: Incoming German Chancellor Friedrich Merz could be left with little room to ease fiscal policy, Berenberg's Holger Schmieding says in a note. The populist AfD and Left parties garnered more than one-third of seats and could block any changes to the German constitution. Potentially they could veto loosening the debt brake which restricts public borrowing. "At a time when it is crucial to raise spending for the military and Ukraine and ease the tax burden for workers and firms, Germany may struggle to find the fiscal space to do so," Schmieding says. A failure to ramp up military spending could get Germany into trouble with Nato partners and risk a trade war with the U.S. should that infuriate President Trump, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

data.title: ECB Might Have to Lower Key Rate to Level That Stimulates Economy, Wunsch Says
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

BRUSSELS — The European Central Bank may have to lower its key rate to a level that stimulates activity if the eurozone economy remains weak and inflation cools, the head of Belgium's central bank said in an interview.

National Bank of Belgium Governor Pierre Wunsch, who is an ECB rate setter, also said a big increase in military spending to reduce Europe's dependence on the U.S. could revive European factories that have too much capacity and too little demand.

ECB policymakers meet next week and are expected to lower the key interest rate for a sixth time since June, to 2.5% from 2.75%. That would bring the key rate closer to the range of 1.75% to 2.25% that economists at the central bank estimate neither spurs nor slows growth.

One debate among policymakers is whether rates would continue to dampen activity to cool inflation after another cut. It is "not clear at all that we would still be restrictive" at that point, Wunsch said.

But he said the bigger question is whether the central bank will need to lower borrowing costs to a level where they are stimulative, or continue to press down on inflation.

"The other question is whether we need to maintain a restrictive stance, move to neutral, or even become slightly supportive," he told The Wall Street Journal. "If inflation falls fast enough and economic weakness persists, we may need to be a bit supportive here."

Investors expect the ECB to continue to lower its key rate beyond next week, which is the likeliest outcome, Wunsch said.

"When I look at market expectations, I'm not shocked. It's very possible we'll see rates move around the 2% mark," he said. "If inflation falls fast enough and economic weakness persists, we may need to be a bit supportive here."

However, Wunsch said policymakers must remain "open-minded on both sides of the equation" and be prepared to respond to "events."

"Directionally, there's probably still room to cut rates, but at some point, we may need to pause if economic activity picks up or if inflation isn't falling as quickly as we thought," he said.

The eurozone's inflation rate has picked up in recent months as energy prices have rebounded, but Wunsch said he had fewer doubts that it will reach the ECB's 2% target than he had earlier some months ago.

"We hope to be close to our target by mid-2025," he said.

While eurozone inflation may be heading in the right direction from the ECB's perspective, economic growth has been weaker than the central bank had anticipated. As in the U.S., the eurozone economy was roiled by the Covid-19 pandemic, but in contrast to the U.S., its recovery was derailed by Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in early 2022.

It faces the threat of further setbacks as President Trump threatens to impose additional tariffs on imports from the bloc, and must ramp up military spending given an increasingly antagonistic U.S. that is moving closer to Russia.

Wunsch said that could help revive Europe's faltering factories, which have struggled to overcome high energy costs and increased competition from China.

"Industry is suffering in Germany, in Belgium, and elsewhere," Wunsch said.

With faltering demand for European goods leaving many factories idling, notably in the continent's auto industry, more investment in arms production could offer a lifeline.

"I'm not saying you can immediately transplant a worker from VW or Audi to Rheinmetall," he said, referring to the German auto giants and a major maker of armoured vehicles and ammunition.

"But it's still industry. Between producing cars and producing tanks, there is some connection. It's not like asking a psychologist to work in the high-tech industry," he said.

Repurposing Europe's factories for defense could help limit the potential inflationary effects of rearmament, Wunsch said.

"If we suddenly spend a huge amount of money in Europe where we don't have capacity, we're going to have inflation without much of a volume impact," he said.

In the meantime, Europe might have shop for weapons in the U.S., Wunsch said, noting that such deals may also be part of trade negotiations with the Trump administration.

"I don't think we can ramp up defense expenditures in Europe to 3%-3.5% [of GDP] very quickly based only on local production," he said. "If we need to ramp up quickly, part of it will have to involve buying American-made equipment."

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: PETRONAS Chemicals' Profit Rockets in Q4 2024
data.description:
Petronas Chemicals' booked a profit attributable to owners of 519 million ringgit in the fourth quarter of 2024, surging from 112 million ringgit a year earlier.

Basic earnings per share jumped to 0.06 ringgit from 0.01 ringgit a year ago, according to a Friday Malaysian bourse filing.

Revenue, however, expanded year over year to 7.46 billion ringgit from 7.21 billion ringgit, the petrochemical products manufacturer said.

The company paid a second interim single-tier dividend of 0.03 ringgit per share, payable on March 20, it noted.

data.title: Zhuzhou Kibing Subsidiaries Complete Asset Sale
data.description:
Zhuzhou Kibing Group subsidiaries Shaoxing Qibin and Zhejiang Energy-Saving Glass completed its 893.5 million yuan asset sale to Chengfa Group.

The assets include land-use rights, buildings and equipment located in Yuecheng District in Shaoxing, China, according to a Feb. 22 filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The transaction is expected to boost the company's first-quarter net profit by about 442 million yuan.

Zhuzhou Kibing Group shares closed 2% higher Monday.

data.title: Formosa Plastics to shut PVC plant for turnaround
data.description: Formosa Plastics Corporation (FPC) is likely to undertake a planned shutdown at its Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) plant in Jen-Wu.A Polymerupdate source in Taiwan informed a team member,

data.title: Nokia Provides Fiber Technology for 25G PON-based Network Deployment in Saudi Arabia
data.description:
Nokia provided the fiber technology for the deployment of Saudi Arabia's first-ever deployment of a 25G PON-based neutral host network.

The open access network deployed by Aces-NH is powered by telecoms equipment giant Nokia's Altiplano Access Controller, according to a Monday release.

data.title: UK 10-Year Gilt Yield Holds Below 4.6%
data.description:
The UK’s 10-year gilt yield remained below 4.6% as investors assessed the German election results and signs of economic stagnation in the UK. In Germany, opposition leader Friedrich Merz’s CDU/CSU bloc won, raising expectations that his government will increase spending and ease strict fiscal policies to boost the economy.

However, the result left mainstream parties without a clear majority, creating uncertainty about rapid policy changes.

In the UK, concerns about stagflation persist. Inflation unexpectedly rose to 3%, a 10-month high, and wages advanced the most since last April.

Meanwhile, February’s S&P Composite PMI dipped slightly to 50.5, indicating stagnation, while employment fell to its lowest level since 2020.

These mixed signals have led traders to price in two Bank of England rate cuts later this year.

data.title: China Expected to Set 2025 GDP Growth Target at 5% Again, UBS Says — Market Talk
data.description: UBS expects Beijing to set this year's GDP growth target at around 5% to manage expectations at the upcoming National People's Congress, UBS economists say in a research note. China's fiscal deficit could rise to 4% of GDP as the government ramps up spending to support consumption and household subsidies. With boosting consumption a top priority, the size of the consumer-goods trade-in program could more than double to over 300 billion yuan, they say. On the monetary side, UBS expects the PBOC to cut policy rates by 30-40 bps later in 2025, likely after the NPC meeting, and anticipates commercial banks will lower deposit rates further. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Kalmar Extends Fleet Maintenance Deal in Singapore
data.description:
Kalmar secured an extension to its cargo-handling fleet and equipment maintenance contract with PSA Singapore.

The Finnish heavy material handling equipment and services company said Monday it entered the deal in the fourth quarter of 2024.

data.title: China stocks drop on worries over Trump's investment curbs
data.description:
China and Hong Kong stocks fell on Monday, dragged down by weakness in tech and healthcare sectors, amid concerns over President Donald Trump's new America First Investment Policy that could escalate tensions between the two biggest economies.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index fell 0.22% and the Shanghai Composite Index dipped 0.18% at close.

** Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 0.58%, while the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index lost 0.55%.

** Tech stocks led the decline in Hong Kong, with the Hang Seng Tech Index dropping 1.2%.

** On Friday, Trump signed a memorandum that directed the Committee on Foreign Investment in the U.S. to restrict Chinese investments in strategic areas, a White House official said.

** The America First Investment Policy will also consider new or expanded restrictions on U.S.-outbound investment to China in sensitive technologies.

** "Investors should not be complacent about escalating U.S.-China competition," Jefferies analysts said after Trump's move.

** The emergence of DeepSeek could prompt the U.S. to be vigilant about setting back China's tech ambitions, they added.

** In mainland A-shares, healthcare and 5G communication sectors (.CSI931079) underperformed, but were partly offset by a rally in property and liquor stocks .

** Hong Kong-listed mainland properties stocks jumped 3.4%.

** George Efstathopoulos, portfolio manager at Fidelity International, said they have been witnessing some green shoots in the property sector in the past few months, indicating that "the worst of the property deleveraging cycle is behind us".

** The smaller Shenzhen index was up 0.13%, the start-up board ChiNext Composite index was weaker by 0.67% and Shanghai's tech-focused STAR50 index (.STAR50) was up 0.48%​.

** Certainly there are some headline risks from Trump's focus on national security and resource nationalism, said Charu Chanana, chief investment strategist at Saxo Markets.

** But the bigger driver for Chinese stocks will continue to be the stimulus measures expected from the annual National People's Congress meeting starting next week, Chanana added.

data.title: $90K Bull market support retest? 5 Things to know in Bitcoin this week
data.description:
Bitcoin heads into the end of February in an uncertain mood — can bulls avoid a fresh $90,000 dip?

  • Liquidity is piling up on either side of the spot price as Bitcoin limps around an increasingly tight trading range.

  • US inflation data is due, including the Fed’s “preferred” index, as stagflation woes deepen.

  • Gold looks set for yet another all-time high, while US dollar strength seeks a turnaround from weeks of downside.

  • Bitcoin implied volatility is sinking to levels rarely seen in its history.

  • Market sentiment is bad, and as network activity drops, analysis warns of trouble brewing.

Traders map out BTC price support toward $90,000

Bitcoin remains stuck in a narrow range, data from Cointelegraph Markets Pro and TradingView shows, after the Bybit hack crushed last week’s attempted upside breakout.

The potential for a fresh push by both bulls and bears, however, is there — as depicted by current liquidity conditions on exchange order books.

“Now we can see in the liquidation levels that the liquidations are equal to the downside or upside,” popular trader CrypNuevo wrote in a thread on X on Feb. 23 while discussing the outlook for the week. 

“Perhaps more to the upside considering price is in a LTF downtrend. $94.7k & $92.5k are key.”

Fellow trader Roman was less optimistic, eyeing a return to the bottom of the multimonth trading range.

“So many failed attempts to move up with a major lack of strength,” he told X followers.

“Just seems like 90k support touch is coming. This invalidates if we break 98.4 with a close above. Range is very tight so I’m expecting a quick move.”

On weekly timeframes, meanwhile, trader Luca eyed an incoming test of Bitcoin’s bull market support band.

This area, formed by two moving averages, had functioned as support since early October when broke through previous all-time highs at $73,800.

Luca suggested that Bitcoin was primed for a return to the upside amid low funding rates, poor sentiment and retail investors reducing exposure.

PCE arrives as “stagflation” concerns fester

Markets are waiting for the “final piece to the puzzle” this week as US inflation data continues to offer risk-asset headwinds.

The Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Index, known to be the Federal Reserve’s “preferred” inflation gauge, is due out on Feb. 28.

It will follow initial jobless claims, which last week beat expectations — demonstrating weakening labor market conditions against a backdrop of resurgent inflation markers. This, as Cointelegraph previously reported, is indicative of “stagflation” — and traders are keeping a close eye on its development.

“The potential for a ‘stagflation’ economic environment is a major concern for investors, where slow economic growth is coupled with high levels of inflation,” trading firm Mosaic Asset wrote in the latest edition of its regular newsletter, “The Market Mosaic,” on Feb. 23. 

“But interestingly, the historical data shows that stagflation isn’t necessarily associated with poor stock market performance.” 

Mosaic noted that out of 12 stagflation years since 1930, the S&P 500 mostly finished higher despite economic pressures.

“Since 1930, there have been 12 years that featured a slowing economy but rising inflation. The stock market’s real return was positive in 75% of those instances, with an average annual real return of 16.4% in the S&P 500,” it reported.

The latest estimates from CME Group’s FedWatch Tool nonetheless underscore markets’ lack of faith in economic policy easing.

Lowering of interest rates, for example, is unlikely before July, despite there being two Fed meetings in the interim.

“PCE inflation will be the final piece to the puzzle as both PPI and CPI inflation are rebounding,” trading resource The Kobeissi Letter meanwhile determined about the upcoming data release, predicting an “eventful” final week of the month.

Gold keeps pushing higher

In contrast to Bitcoin and altcoins, one asset is refusing to take any notice of fluctuating economic uncertainty: gold.

The precious metal continues to set new all-time highs and, as of Feb. 24, is working on its highest-ever daily close.

The US dollar index (DXY), which measures dollar strength against a basket of US trading partner currencies, is meanwhile seeking a reversal from a downtrend in place since early December.

While a strong dollar typically pressures risk assets across the board, Kobeissi notes that the current landscape stands out on longer timeframes.

“Since late-July, gold prices are up ~24% while the US Dollar is up ~2% and the 10-year note yield is up ~8%,” it noted in a dedicated X thread on the topic last week. 

“While gold and rates/USD typically have an inverse correlation, they are rising TOGETHER.”

Kobeissi described surging gold demand across the world, calling it the “global safe haven asset” amid worries over US trade policy and tariffs.

“What's even more impressive is that gold is rising WITH the S&P 500,” it continued. 

“In fact, gold has more than DOUBLED the S&P 500's YTD return. In 2024, gold and the S&P 500 had an unprecedented correlation of ~0.81.”

As Cointelegraph reported, Bitcoin has often been seen to copy gold bull run moves with a delay of around three months.

“Gold is ripping, Bitcoin chopping. Summer 2024 vibes,” Charles Edwards, founder of quantitative Bitcoin and digital asset fund Capriole Investments, summarized to X followers earlier this month. 

“You know the deal, Bitcoin will bore you to death. As long as Gold keeps trending, Bitcoin almost always sees a comparable (bigger) breakout within 3-6 months.”

Bitcoin volatility metric challenges records

Bitcoin’s stubborn trading range has led to some rare readings from volatility metrics.

On weekly timeframes, realized volatility, which measures the standard deviation of market returns from its mean, is near record lows.

The trend was noticed by onchain analytics firm Glassnode at the weekend.

“Bitcoin’s 1-week realized volatility has collapsed to 23.42%, nearing historical lows. In the past four years, it has dipped lower only a few times - e.g., Oct 2024 (22.88%) & Nov 2023 (21.35%),” it revealed in an X thread. 

“Similar compressions in the past led to major market moves.”

Glassnode drew similar conclusions from 1-week options realized volatility, now approaching multi-year lows.

“The last time IV was this low (2023, early 2024), major volatility spikes followed. Meanwhile, longer-term IV remains higher (3m: 53.1%, 6m: 56.25%),” it reported.

Low volatility has long been on the radar among Bitcoin traders thanks to the inability of either buyers or sellers to effect a lasting trend change.

Network activity sees a slow bleed

Bitcoin network activity and sentiment are both declining — and analysis warns that this could have long-term implications for price action.

Active wallet addresses continue to shrink in the aftermath of the US Presidential Election, leading onchain analytics platform CryptoQuant to draw comparisons to BTC price dips earlier in the current bull run.

“Additionally, the accumulation rate of Bitcoin spot ETFs has slowed, with recent minor capital outflows observed,” contributor Avocado_onchain wrote in one of its “Quicktake” blog posts on Feb. 23.

“The number of UTXOs is also decreasing, with the magnitude of the decline similar to the correction period in September 2023. If this trend continues, we could see signs of investor exodus similar to the market cycle peak of 2017.”

While the post acknowledged that “a simple decline in UTXOs alone is not enough to confirm the end of the current cycle,” flagging sentiment remains a problem.

The Crypto Fear & Greed Index currently measures 49/100, circling “neutral” territory after remaining flat throughout most of February.

“Since previous bullish narratives have already been priced in, further upside momentum will require either the resolution of uncertainties or new bullish catalysts,” Avocado_onchain summarized.

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: Corporate bosses urge Berlin to quickly move on weak economy
data.description:
German business leaders on Monday called on Berlin to quickly form a new government, warning Europe's top economy could not afford to waste any time as companies suffer from high costs, red tape and rising competition from abroad.

The country's conservative CDU/CSU bloc won Sunday's national election to pave the way for a coalition with the Social Democrats, easing concerns over a more fractious three-way government and prompting the corporate call for the parties to move swiftly.

Germany's top companies have long criticised the government's lack of action over energy costs, among the highest in Europe and more than twice as high as in China and the United States, as well as bureaucracy.

"Germany now needs a government that is able and willing to act - and quickly," said Christian Sewing, CEO of Germany's largest lender Deutsche Bank and president of the association of German banks.

"The challenges facing our country are enormous: The economy urgently needs a fresh start with fundamental reforms."

A recent survey by Germany's Chamber of Commerce and Industry showed 60% of firms citing economic conditions, including bureaucracy and high labour and energy costs, as the biggest business risks, the highest level ever.

This has fuelled fears of deindustrialisation as companies opt to invest outside Germany where conditions are more favourable.

Christian Bruch, CEO of power equipment supplier Siemens Energy , said Germany needed to quickly regain its competitiveness and the country's democratic centrist parties would need to form a coalition as fast as possible.

"Actions in energy policy are crucial for this. The expansion of gas-fired power plants, the strengthening of wind energy, and the modernisation of electricity grids are essential, as is a secure supply of raw materials."

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0841 GMT - The euro is unlikely to rise much further following Sunday's German election, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. The euro could continue to react as coalition talks start, especially regarding the debt rule, he says. However, German politics will likely soon return to being a small secondary factor for the euro, with the currency driven primarily by U.S. tariff news and U.S.-Russia-Ukraine peace talks. The looming risk of U.S. tariffs and the European Central Bank's loose policy stance should limit euro gains. "Ultimately, we expect to see [the euro] return below $1.04 over the next four weeks." The euro is last up 0.2% at $1.0476. It hit a four-week high of $1.0531 overnight, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - Europe should forge its own policy on China without relying on direction from the U.S., Spain's chief diplomat says. "Europe must take its own decisions, on its own. And we have to decide when China can be a partner and when China is a competitor," Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares tells the Financial Times in an interview. His comments come amid a major shift in geopolitical and trade relations between the U.S. and the rest of the world under President Trump. A good relationship between Europe and China is vital given the country's size and importance for issues like climate change, Albares says. "We need to have an engagement," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0822 GMT - The euro rises after Sunday's German federal election, although the outcome isn't a big deal for the currency market, Commerzbank currency analyst Ulrich Leuchtmann says in a note. As suggested by polls ahead of the election, a coalition between the CDU/CSU alliance and the Social Democrats looks feasible, he says. "The fact that anything euro positive emerged at all is rather surprising and shows that there was a residual fear in the currency market of a destructive result." The euro is also helped by a weaker dollar, he says. The euro rises 0.2% to $1.0485, having hit a four-week high of $1.0531 overnight, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Investor spirits have been lifted on the hope that the election win by Germany's conservatives will pull the country out of an economic stupor and bolster collective defense spending, Susannah Streeter, head of money and markets at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. Friedrich Merz, the leader of the CDU/CSU party, has pledged to relax fiscal rules, increase defense spending and boost the economy with much needed investment, she writes. With Russia's aggression in Europe set to remain an unrelenting black cloud, political factions may pull together to boost contributions to NATO, she adds. The German DAX trades up 0.3% at 22359 points. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0818 GMT - RBC Capital Markets expects that German Bund yields will rise near term as bonds are impacted by a looser fiscal stance in Germany, which could come about under a new government after Germany's election. "In the near term, we hold a bearish call and have been short Bunds for quite some time," RBC analysts say in a note. Ten-year Bund yields briefly rose above 2.50% last week and RBC expects them to rise back above that level soon. They also see potential for a further rise towards 2.75% going into spring. A more business-friendly government and a looser fiscal stance should lift economic expectations, which is negative for bond markets, they say. The 10-year Bund yield is last up 1 basis point at 2.475%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - Chinese policymakers are likely to trim their GDP growth target to around 4.5% to 5.0%, down from around 5% last year, due to greater external uncertainty, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. External demand may drag on growth this year, with rising geopolitical tensions since Trump's second administration. Beijing may acknowledge these external and domestic challenges and emphasize its commitment to stabilizing growth, they add. The analysts note that the 10% across-the-board tariffs on China, enacted since February 4, align with their base case However, there is a possibility of further tariffs as Trump's "America First" trade executive order requires certain departments to publish trade reports by April 1, potentially leading to additional trade actions against China, they say.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0803 GMT - Dollar purchases ahead of month-end rebalancing of portfolios look weak, Barclays analysts say in a note. The dollar has come off its recent peak as positive sentiment surrounding the currency has retraced, they say. This largely increases asset values in dollars outside the U.S. and raises the need to buy dollars at month-end for rebalancing. The outperformance of European equities compared to the U.S. and appreciation of the Japanese yen against the dollar "contributed to a moderate dollar-buying signal against both currencies at month-end," they say. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 106.409 after hitting a two-and-a-half-month low of 106.126 overnight. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - The new German parliament will find it difficult reform the debt brake and free up spending on defense or infrastructure, Deutsche Bank economists say in a note. Together the CDU/CSU, SPD and Greens hold just under the required two-thirds majority for constitutional changes, meaning any change to the debt brake would rely on the support of the Left or AfD, they say. "This could be seen as a negative by markets in terms of reducing the likelihood of a decisive fiscal regime shift in Germany," the economists say. However, while the Left campaigned for lowering defense spending, they are in favor of higher infrastructure investments, so there could be cross-party consensus on freeing up more funds, they note. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - German government bond yields edge higher in early trade as markets welcome the results of Sunday's election. The Christian Democrats made a convincing victory, positioning Friedrich Merz as the likely next Chancellor. The next government is most likely to be a Grand coalition between the Christian Democratic Union and its Bavarian sister party CSU alongside the Social Democrats. The 10-year Bund yield rises 1 basis points to 2.473%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - The European economy would benefit if the European Central Bank were to cut its key interest rate below the neutral level--estimated by the ECB at around 2%--, says Mediolanum International Funds Ltd's Marco Meijer. In the near term, a more fragmented global economy and rising deficits are unlikely to provide a significant boost to growth, which should put downward pressure on Bund yields, bringing them closer to 2% by the summer, he says. It remains uncertain whether the ECB's key rate will fall below 2%, however. "There is not yet a clear commitment for it to go below that level," the deputy head of fixed income says. The 10-year Bund yield rises 0.5 basis points to 2.468%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - A fresh start for Germany looks less likely after the center-right won less convincingly than had been expected, Joerg Kraemer at Commerzbank writes in a note to investors. The conservatives under Friedrich Merz came out on top in Sunday's parliamentary elections but Merz faces long and tough negotiations to form a government with other parties. A coalition with the outgoing centre-left party looks likeliest but the two parties would have only a slim majority in the Bundestag. And while the two parties would likely agree on an initial cash boost for infrastructure, their differences outweigh their similarities, Kraemer says. "This dampens the hope that there will be a fresh start in economic policy," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0731 GMT - A decisive fiscal regime shift in Germany seems unlikely after centrist parties failed to win a constitutional majority, analysts at Deutsche Bank write. Even with the support of the green party, the conservative party CDU/CSU and Chancellor Olaf Scholz's Social Democratic Party jointly hold just under 66% of seats, they add. Constitutional changes, including to the country's fiscal rules, known as debt brake, would rely on the support of far-left Die Linke or far-right AfD, the analysts write. This could be seen as negative by investors as its reduces the likelihood of a decisive shift in Germany's fiscal framework, the analysts add. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Euro Gains Are Likely to Remain Limited After German Election — Market Talk
data.description: The euro is unlikely to rise much further following Sunday's German election, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. The euro could continue to react as coalition talks start, especially regarding the debt rule, he says. However, German politics will likely soon return to being a small secondary factor for the euro, with the currency driven primarily by U.S. tariff news and U.S.-Russia-Ukraine peace talks. The looming risk of U.S. tariffs and the European Central Bank's loose policy stance should limit euro gains. "Ultimately, we expect to see [the euro] return below $1.04 over the next four weeks." The euro is last up 0.2% at $1.0476. It hit a four-week high of $1.0531 overnight, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)
